chunk_summary: Pediatric CKD diet prescriptions prioritize growth by meeting energy and protein needs, with lab-driven restrictions for electrolytes and minerals. Dialysis-specific adjustments and frequent reviews are essential for high-risk children.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of growth-first nutrition in pediatric CKD management.
Learn how to apply lab-driven restrictions for electrolytes and minerals in CKD patients.
Identify dialysis-specific dietary adjustments for protein and energy needs.
Recognize the role of frequent nutritional reviews in high-risk pediatric CKD cases.
key_facts: Energy and protein intake should target the higher end of age-based ranges when growth is poor.
Dialysis-specific protein adjustments account for losses, with peritoneal and hemodialysis differing in allowances.
Electrolyte and mineral management should be based on trends in calcium, phosphate, PTH, alkaline phosphatase, and 25-hydroxyvitamin D levels.
Potassium restriction should only be applied for documented hyperkalemia trends to avoid reducing diet quality.
High-risk children, such as those on dialysis or with poor growth, require monthly or more frequent reviews.
In India, standardized growth-first protocols have reduced preventable growth failure in pediatric CKD patients.
reference_quote: Pediatric CKD nutrition should start with meeting energy and protein needs for growth, then apply lab-linked restrictions only when abnormalities persist.
document_key_topics: Frequent nutritional reviews for high-risk children
